{{Short description|Belief or behavior that is considered irrational or supernatural}}
{{Other uses}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=October 2023}}
{{multiple issues|
{{Fringe theories|date=October 2023}}
{{cleanup rewrite|date=October 2023}}
}}
{{Anthropology of religion|Basic}}
[[File:Salem witch2.jpg|thumb|[[Witch-hunt]]ing is commonly motivated by religious superstition;{{citation needed|date=July 2024}} pictured is an imaginative depiction of the [[Salem Witch Trials]]|240x240px]]
A '''superstition''' is any [[belief]] or practice considered by non-practitioners to be [[irrational]] or [[supernatural]]. It is commonly applied to beliefs and practices surrounding [[luck]], [[fate]], [[magic (supernatural)|magic]], [[amulet]]s, [[astrology]], [[fortune telling]], [[Spirit (animating force)|spirits]], and certain [[paranormal]] [[wikt: entity|entities]], particularly the belief that future events can be foretold by specific unrelated prior events.<ref name =vyse21>{{cite book|last=Vyse|first=Stuart A.|author-link=Stuart Vyse|title=Believing in Magic: The Psychology of Superstition|year=2000|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=Oxford, England|isbn=978-0-1951-3634-0|pages=19–22}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last=Chardonnens|first=L. S.|url=https://brill.com/view/book/9789047420422/Bej.9789004158290.i-608_006.xml|title=Chapter Four. Superstition and prognostication|year= 2007|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-90-474-2042-2|language=en|access-date=28 December 2020|archive-date=18 January 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210118173448/https://brill.com/view/book/9789047420422/Bej.9789004158290.i-608_006.xml|url-status=live}}</ref>

{{Paranormal}}

The word ''superstition'' is also used to refer to a [[religion]] not practiced by the majority of a given society regardless of whether the prevailing religion contains alleged superstitions or to all religions by the [[antireligion|antireligious]].<ref name="vyse21" />

== Contemporary use ==
Definitions of the term vary, but superstitions are commonly described as irrational beliefs at odds with scientific knowledge of the world. [[Stuart Vyse]] proposes that a superstition's "presumed mechanism of action is inconsistent with our understanding of the physical world", with Jane Risen adding that these beliefs are not merely scientifically wrong but impossible.<ref name=VS>{{Cite book |last=Vyse |first=Stuart |title=Superstition: A Very Short Introduction |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=2020 |isbn=978-0198819257}}</ref><ref name=Risen/> Similarly, Lysann Damisch defines superstition as "irrational  beliefs that an object, action, or circumstance that is not logically related to a course of events influences its outcome."<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Damisch|first1=Lysann|last2=Stoberock|first2=Barbara|last3=Mussweiler|first3=Thomas|date=1 July 2010|title=Keep Your Fingers Crossed!: How Superstition Improves Performance|url=https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797610372631|journal=Psychological Science|language=en|volume=21|issue=7|pages=1014–1020|doi=10.1177/0956797610372631|pmid=20511389|s2cid=8862493|issn=0956-7976|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Tosyali|first1=Furkan|last2=Aktas|first2=Busra|date=1 December 2021|title=Does training analytical thinking decrease superstitious beliefs? Relationship between analytical thinking, intrinsic religiosity, and superstitious beliefs|url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886921005018|journal=Personality and Individual Differences|language=en|volume=183|article-number=111122|doi=10.1016/j.paid.2021.111122|s2cid=237658088 |issn=0191-8869|url-access=subscription|hdl=20.500.12723/3083|hdl-access=free}}</ref> [[Dale Martin (scholar)|Dale Martin]] says that superstitions "presuppose an erroneous understanding about cause and effect, that have been rejected by modern science."<ref name=":6">{{Cite book|last=Martin|first=Dale B.|author-link=Dale Martin (scholar)|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fjTU6YmauZwC|title=Inventing Superstition|year=2009|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-04069-4|pages=10–20|language=en}}</ref> The ''[[Oxford English Dictionary]]''<ref name=OED>{{Cite web|date=September 2021|title=superstition, n.|url=https://www.oed.com/view/Entry/194517|access-date=28 November 2021|website=OED Online|publisher=[[Oxford University Press]]|language=en}}</ref> describes them as "irrational, unfounded", [[Merriam-Webster]] as "a false conception about causation or belief or practice",<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|title=Definition of SUPERSTITION|url=https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/superstition|access-date=30 December 2020|website=www.merriam-webster.com|language=en|archive-date=7 October 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081007152508/https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/superstition|url-status=live}}</ref> and the [[Cambridge Advanced Learner's Dictionary|Cambridge Dictionary]] as "sans grounding in human reason or scientific knowledge".<ref>{{Cite web|title=Superstition meaning in the Cambridge English Dictionary|url=https://dictionary.cambridge.org/dictionary/english/superstition|access-date=30 December 2020|website=dictionary.cambridge.org|language=en|archive-date=8 November 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181108184349/https://dictionary.cambridge.org/dictionary/english/superstition|url-status=live}}</ref> This notion of superstitious practices is not causally related to the outcomes.<ref name=":7">{{Cite journal|last1=Carlson|first1=Brad D.|last2=Mowen|first2=John C.|last3=Fang|first3=Xiang|date=2009|title=Trait superstition and consumer behavior: Re-conceptualization, measurement, and initial investigations|url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/mar.20295|journal=Psychology & Marketing|language=en|volume=26|issue=8|pages=691 of 689–713|doi=10.1002/mar.20295|issn=1520-6793|access-date=3 January 2021|archive-date=18 January 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210118174412/https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/mar.20295|url-status=live|url-access=subscription}}</ref> <!-- This journal article has been cited by fifty plus consumer behavior related journals and John C. Mowen seems to have been used as ref in some of other wikipedia articles earlier -->

Both Vyse and Martin argue that what is considered superstitious varies across cultures and time. For Vyse, "if a culture has not yet adopted science as its standard, then what we consider magic or superstition is more accurately the local science or religion."<ref name=VS/> Dale points out that superstitions are often considered out of place in modern times and are influenced by modern science and its notions of what is rational or irrational, surviving as remnants of older popular beliefs and practices.<ref name=":3"/>

Vyse proposes that in addition to being irrational and culturally dependent, superstitions have to be instrumental; an actual effect is expected by the person holding a belief, such as increased odds of winning a prize. This distinction excludes practices where participants merely expect to be entertained.<ref name=VS/>

==Alternative religious beliefs as superstition==
{{See also|folk religion|lived religion|Superstitions in Muslim societies|Evolutionary origin of religions|Evolutionary psychology of religion}}

Religious practices that differ from commonly accepted religions in a given culture are sometimes called superstitious; similarly, new practices brought into an established religious community can also be labeled as superstitious in an attempt to exclude them. Also, an excessive display of devoutness has often been labelled as superstitious behavior.<ref name="vyse21"/><ref>{{Cite web|title=Superstition|url=https://dictionary.cambridge.org/dictionary/english/superstition|access-date=30 December 2020|website=dictionary.cambridge.org|language=en|archive-date=8 November 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181108184349/https://dictionary.cambridge.org/dictionary/english/superstition|url-status=live}}</ref>{{qn|date=September 2024}}<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Selberg |first=Torunn |date=December 2003 |title=Taking Superstitions Seriously |url =https://www.jstor.org/stable/pdf/30035120.pdf |journal=Folklore |volume=114 |issue=3 |pages=297–306 |doi =10.1080/0015587032000145342 |jstor=30035120 |s2cid=145299302}}</ref>{{qn|date=September 2024}}

In antiquity, the Latin term {{lang | la | superstitio}}, like its equivalent Greek ''deisidaimonia'', became associated with exaggerated ritual and a credulous attitude towards prophecies.<ref>{{Cite book |last =Scheid |first =John |chapter =Superstitio |title=Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Classics |year= 2016 |chapter-url =https://oxfordre.com/classics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780199381135.001.0001/acrefore-9780199381135-e-6150 |access-date=1 July 2022  |doi =10.1093/acrefore/9780199381135.013.6150 |isbn =978-0-19-938113-5 |archive-date=1 July 2022 |archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20220701234108/https://oxfordre.com/classics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780199381135.001.0001/acrefore-9780199381135-e-6150 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name=OED/><ref name =vyse21/> Greek and Roman [[Polytheism|polytheists]], who modeled their relations with the gods on political and social terms, scorned the man who constantly trembled with fear at the thought of the gods, as a slave feared a cruel and capricious master. Such fear of the gods was what the Romans meant by "superstition" (Veyne 1987, p.&nbsp;211). [[Cicero]] (106–43 BCE) contrasted {{lang | la | superstitio}} with the [[mainstream religion]] of his day, stating: {{lang | la | Nec vero superstitione tollenda religio tollitur}} – "One does not destroy religion by destroying superstition".<ref>
''De Divinatione'', Book 2, chapter 72, section 148.
</ref>
Diderot's 18th-century ''[[Encyclopédie]]'' defines superstition as "any excess of religion in general", and links it specifically with [[paganism]].<ref>{{Cite journal |url=http://quod.lib.umich.edu/cgi/t/text/text-idx?c=did;cc=did;rgn=main;view=text;idno=did2222.0000.629 |title=Superstition |journal =Encyclopedia of Diderot & d'Alembert – Collaborative Translation Project |access-date =1 April 2015 |date =10 October 2010 |last1 =Louis |first1 =Chevalier de Jaucourt (Biography) |archive-date=19 November 2015 |archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20151119060008/http://quod.lib.umich.edu/cgi/t/text/text-idx?c=did;cc=did;rgn=main;view=text;idno=did2222.0000.629 |url-status =live }}</ref>
<!--
this is a mistake by Chambers, perhaps interesting for [[Desdemona]], but irrelevant here:
 (''[[Desdemona#Etymology|deisidaimonia]]''<ref name="Martin2004">{{cite book |last=Martin |first=Dale B. |title=Inventing superstition: from the Hippocratics to the Christians |url=https://archive.org/details/inventingsuperst00mart |url-access=limited |year=2004 |publisher=Harvard University Press |location=Cambridge, MA |isbn=0-674-01534-7 |page=[https://archive.org/details/inventingsuperst00mart/page/n92 80]}}</ref><ref name=chambers>{{cite web |last=The Earl of Shaftesbury |first=Antony Cooper |title=Chambers's Cyclopaedia of English Literature, Volume 2 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pc8iAAAAMAAJ&q=%22denoted+the+lady+superstitious+(deisidaimonia%22|work=Advice to an Author |publisher=W. & R. Chambers, Limited |access-date=28 March 2011 |author=Robert Chambers |coauthors=David Patrick (New Edition) |location=London & Edinburgh |page=170 |language=en |year=1902 |quote=[Beginning with a quote from Othello, Act 1. Scene III:] 'This to hear Would Desdemona seriously incline.' ...But why, amongst his Greek names, he should have chosen one [that] denoted the lady superstitious (deisidaimonia in Greek means superstition) I can't imagine...}}</ref>)
-->

In his 1520 ''[[Prelude on the Babylonian Captivity of the Church]]'', [[Martin Luther]], who called the [[papacy]] "that fountain and source of all superstitions", accuses the popes of superstition:
<blockquote>For there was scarce another of the celebrated bishoprics that had so few learned pontiffs; only in violence, intrigue, and superstition has it hitherto surpassed the rest. For the men who occupied the Roman&nbsp;See a thousand years ago differ so vastly from those who have since come into power, that one is compelled to refuse the name of Roman pontiff either to the former or to the latter.<ref name="Steinhaeuser1915">{{citation |mode =cs1 |url =https://books.google.com/books?id=Q3xIAAAAMAAJ |title=Works of Martin&nbsp;Luther: With Instructions and Notes |date =1915 |page=291 |chapter =The Babylonian Captivity §&nbsp;The Sacrament of Extreme Unction |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q3xIAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA291 |last1 = Luther |first1 =Martin |editor1-last =Jacobs |editor1-first =Henry Eyster |editor2-last =Spaeth |editor2-first =Adolph |translator-last =Steinhaeuser |translator-first =Albert&nbsp;T.&nbsp;W. |publisher =A.&nbsp;J. Holman Company |location =Philadelphia |volume =2 | isbn =9780722221235 |lccn =15007839 |oclc =300541097 |quote =For there was scarce another of the celebrated bishoprics that had so few learned pontiffs; only in violence, intrigue, and superstition has it hitherto surpassed the rest. For the men who occupied the Roman&nbsp;See a thousand years ago differ so vastly from those who have since come into power, that one is compelled to refuse the name of Roman pontiff either to the former or to the latter.}}</ref></blockquote>

The current ''[[Catechism of the Catholic Church]]'' considers superstition [[sin]]ful in the sense that it denotes "a perverse excess of religion", as a demonstrated lack of [[trust (sociology)|trust]] in [[divine providence]]&nbsp;(¶ 2110), and a violation of the first of the [[Ten Commandments]].
The ''Catechism'' represents a defense against the accusation that Catholic [[doctrine]] is superstitious:
<blockquote>Superstition is a deviation of religious feeling and of the practices this feeling imposes. It can even affect the worship we offer the true God, e.g., when one attributes an importance in some way magical to certain practices otherwise lawful or necessary. To attribute the efficacy of prayers or of sacramental signs to their mere external performance, apart from the interior dispositions that they demand is to fall into superstition. Cf. ''{{bibleverse|Matthew|23:16–22}}''&nbsp;(¶ 2111)</blockquote>

[[File:WeirdTalesv36n2pg043 Superstitions and Taboos.png|thumb|Examples of superstitions and taboos from a November 1941 issue of ''[[Weird Tales]]'']]

=== Classifications ===
Dieter Harmening's 1979 book ''Superstitio'' categorizes superstitions in three categories: [[Magic (supernatural)|magic]], [[divination]] and observances.<ref>
{{cite book
 |last1                = Harmening
 |first1               = Dieter
 |year                 = 1979
 |chapter              =
 |title                = Superstitio: Überlieferungs- und theoriegeschichtliche Untersuchungen zur kirchlich-theologischen Aberglaubensliteratur des Mittelalters
 |url                  = https://books.google.com/books?id=ITDjAtgNqWUC
 |language             = German
 |volume               = 1
 |publication-place    = Berlin
 |publisher            = Erich Schmidt Verlag GmbH & Co KG
 |page                 =
 |isbn                 = 9783503012916
 |quote                =
}}
</ref>{{pn|date=September 2024}}
The observances category subdivides into  [[omen|"signs"]] and "time".<ref name=":2" />{{qn|date=September 2024}} The time sub-category constitutes temporal prognostics like observances of various days related like [[dog days]], [[Egyptian days]], year prognosis and [[Lunary|lunaries]], whereas the signs category constitutes signs such as particular animal behaviors (like the call of birds or the neighing of horses) or the sighting of comets, or dreams.<ref name=":2" /> According to László Sándor Chardonnens, the signs subcategory usually needs an observer who might help in interpreting the signs and such observer does not need necessarily to be an active participant in the observation.<ref name=":2" /><ref>
Compare:
{{cite book
 |last1                = Chardonnens
 |first1               = László Sándor
 |year                 = 2007
 |chapter              = Superstition and Prognostication
 |title                = Anglo-Saxon Prognostics, 900–1100: Study and Texts
 |url                  = https://books.google.com/books?id=CZS6CsIkCRoC
 |series               = Volume 153 of Brill's studies in intellectual history: Brill's Texts and Sources in Intellectual History, volume 3
 |publication-place    = Leiden
 |publisher            = Brill
 |page                 = 105
 |isbn                 = 9789004158290
 |access-date          = 29 September 2024
 |quote                = [...] the observation of signs and times simply entails observibng properly and interpreting the results. [...] This branch of superstition requires an observer who interprets the findings, but this observer need not participate in any activity to disclose that which is to be observed.
}}
</ref>
According to Chardonnens, a participant in the category of divination may need to go beyond mere observation and need to be active participant in a given action.<ref name=":2" /> Examples of divination superstitions include [[judicial astrology]], [[necromancy]], [[haruspex]], [[Cleromancy|lot-casting]], [[geomancy]], [[aeromancy]] and [[prophecy]].<ref name=":2" /> Chardonnens says superstitions belonging to the magic category are exceedingly hermetical and ritualistic: examples include witchcraft, potions, [[incantation]]s, amulets etc.<ref name=":2" /> Chardonnens says that the observation category needs an observer, divination category needs a participant to tell what is to be observed, whereas magic requires a participant who must follow a protocol to influence the future, and that these three types of superstition need increasing stages of participation and knowledge.<ref name=":2" />

Chardonnens defines "prognostication" as that component of superstition which expects knowledge of the future on systematic application of given ritual and order,<ref name=":2" /><ref>
{{cite book
 |last1                = Chardonnens
 |first1               = László Sándor
 |year                 = 2007
 |chapter              = Superstition and Prognostication
 |title                = Anglo-Saxon Prognostics, 900–1100: Study and Texts
 |url                  = https://books.google.com/books?id=CZS6CsIkCRoC
 |series               = Volume 153 of Brill's studies in intellectual history: Brill's Texts and Sources in Intellectual History, volume 3
 |publication-place    = Leiden
 |publisher            = Brill
 |page                 = 103
 |isbn                 = 9789004158290
 |access-date          = 29 September 2024
 |quote                = [...] medieval prognosticatory superstition, which I describe as a aystem which, if properly applied, yields knowledge of the future. This working definition asserts that prognostication is a component of superstition. [...] Moreover, the working definition makes clear that prognostication is systematic, not random, and that it relies on ritual and order.
}}
</ref>
and moves to classify it, writing: "Prognostication seems to occupy a place somewhere between observation and divination, of which the observation of times is represented most frequently due to the primacy of temporal prognostics.<ref name=":2" /><ref>
{{cite book
 |last1                = Chardonnens
 |first1               = László Sándor
 |year                 = 2007
 |chapter              = Superstition and Prognostication
 |title                = Anglo-Saxon Prognostics, 900–1100: Study and Texts
 |url                  = https://books.google.com/books?id=CZS6CsIkCRoC
 |series               = Volume 153 of Brill's studies in intellectual history: Brill's Texts and Sources in Intellectual History, volume 3
 |publication-place    = Leiden
 |publisher            = Brill
 |page                 = 107
 |isbn                 = 9789004158290
 |access-date          = 29 September 2024
 |quote                = Prognostication seems to occupy a place somewhere between observation and divination, of which the observation of times is represented most frequently due to the primacy of temporal prognostics.
}}
</ref><ref>
{{cite book
 |last1                = Chardonnens
 |first1               = László Sándor
 |year                 = 2007
 |chapter              = Superstition and Prognostication
 |title                = Anglo-Saxon Prognostics, 900–1100: Study and Texts
 |url                  = https://books.google.com/books?id=CZS6CsIkCRoC
 |series               = Volume 153 of Brill's studies in intellectual history: Brill's Texts and Sources in Intellectual History, volume 3
 |publication-place    = Leiden
 |publisher            = Brill
 |page                 = 105
 |isbn                 = 9789004158290
 |access-date          = 29 September 2024
 |quote                = The group of observation of times contains all temporal prognostics, which make up the larger part of the English prognostic corpus.
}}
</ref>

Chardonnens classifies prophecy under the topic of divination; examples including the  [[biblical prophets|prophets]] of the [[Old Testament]], biblical  [[Typology (theology)|typological]] allegory, the [[Fifteen Signs before Doomsday|fifteen signs before Judgement Day]], and the many prophecies expressed by [[saints]]; Chardonnens further points out that since many aspects of religious experience are tied up with prophecy, the medieval  [[Latin Church|church]] condones the same.<ref name=":2" /><ref>
{{cite book
 |last1                = Chardonnens
 |first1               = László Sándor
 |year                 = 2007
 |chapter              = Superstition and Prognostication
 |title                = Anglo-Saxon Prognostics, 900–1100: Study and Texts
 |url                  = https://books.google.com/books?id=CZS6CsIkCRoC
 |series               = Volume 153 of Brill's studies in intellectual history: Brill's Texts and Sources in Intellectual History, volume 3
 |publication-place    = Leiden
 |publisher            = Brill
 |page                 = 108
 |isbn                 = 9789004158290
 |access-date          = 29 September 2024
 |quote                = Prophecy is a type of divination which is condoned by the church, since many aspects of the religious experience are tied up with prophecy. Examples include the prophets of the Old Testament, biblical typological allegory, the fifteen signs before Judgement Day, and the many prophecies uttered by saints.
}}
</ref>
Chardonnens says, one could differentiate between those kinds of prophecy which are (1) inspired by God or Satan and their minions; (2) "gecyndelic"; and (3) "wiglung" examples —lacking divine or infernal inspiration and not "gecyndelic" either. But practically, however, most, if not all, words relating to prophecy ought to be interpreted as inspired.<ref name=":2" />

=== Criticism of definitions ===
Identifying something as ''superstition'' generally expresses a pejorative view. Items referred to as such in common parlance are commonly referred to as [[folk belief]] in [[folkloristics]].<ref name="FOLKLORISTICS">For discussion, see for example Georges, Robert A. & Jones, Michael Owen. 1995. ''Folkloristics: An Introduction'', p. 122. [[Indiana University Press]]. {{ISBN|0253329345}}.</ref>

According to László Sándor Chardonnens, [[Oxford English Dictionary]] (OED) definitions pass value-judgement and attribution to "fear and ignorance" without doing enough justice to elaborate systems of superstitions.<ref name=":2" /> Chardonnens says the religious element in OED denotations is not understood as a system of observance and testifies to a belief in higher power on part of the compiler of the dictionary.<ref name=":2" />

==== Subjective perceptions ====
Richard Webster's ''The Encyclopedia of Superstitions'' points out that many superstitions have connections with religion, that people may hold individual subjective perceptions ''vis à vis'' superstitions against one another (people of one belief are likely to call people of another belief superstitious); Constantine regarded paganism as a superstition; Tacitus on other hand regarded Christianity as a pernicious superstition; [[Paul the Apostle|Saul of Tarsus]] and [[Martin Luther]] perceived any thing that was not centered on Christ to be superstitious.<ref name=":5">{{Cite book|last =Webster|first =Richard|url =https://books.google.com/books?id=m1-xCL47_7QC|title =The Encyclopedia of Superstitions|publisher =Llewellyn Worldwide|year = 2012|isbn =978-0-7387-2561-1|page =xi |publication-place = Woodbury, Minnesota |language =en | quote = There are many superstitions connected with religion, and people who belong to one faith are likely to consider people with different beliefs superstitious. Constantine considered paganism a superstition. Tacitus, on the other hand, considered Christianity a pernicious superstition. Martin Luther said that anything that does not center on Christ was superstition. St. Paul also believed this [...].}}</ref> According to Dale Martin, difference of opinion on what constitutes "superstition" may become apparent when one moves from one culture to another culture.<ref>
{{cite book
 |last1                = Martin
 |first1               = Dale Basil
 |author-link1         = Dale Martin (scholar)
 |date                 = 1 July 2009
 |chapter              = Problems of Definition
 |title                = Inventing Superstition: From the Hippocratics to the Christians
 |url                  = https://books.google.com/books?id=fjTU6YmauZwC
 |publisher            = Harvard University Press
 |page                 = 11
 |isbn                 = 9780674040694
 |access-date          = 29 September 2024
 |quote                = Disagreements about what counts as 'superstition' will usually be exacerbated when we move from one culture to another.
}}
</ref>

===Etymology===
While the formation of the Latin word is clear, from the verb ''[[:wikt:supersto#Latin|super-stare]]'', "to stand over, stand upon; survive", its original intended sense is less clear. It can be interpreted as "'standing over a thing in amazement or awe",<ref>"orig. a standing still over or by a thing; hence, amazement, wonder, dread, esp. of the divine or supernatural." Charlton T. Lewis, Charles Short, [https://www.perseus.tufts.edu/hopper/text?doc=superstitio&fromdoc=Perseus%3Atext%3A1999.04.0059 A Latin Dictionary] {{Webarchive|url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200808040852/http://www.perseus.tufts.edu/hopper/text?doc=superstitio&fromdoc=Perseus%3Atext%3A1999.04.0059 |date =8 August 2020 }}.</ref> but other possibilities have been suggested, e.g. the sense of ''excess'', i.e. over-scrupulousness or over-ceremoniousness in the performing of religious rites, or else the ''survival'' of old, irrational religious habits.<ref>{{cite book|title =Oxford Latin Dictionary|publisher =Oxford University Press|year =1982|location =Oxford, England}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last =Turcan|first =Robert|url=https://archive.org/details/cultsofromanempi00robe|title =The Cults of the Roman Empire|publisher =Blackwell|others =Nevill, Antonia (trans.)|year=1996|isbn=978-0-631-20047-5|location=Oxford, England|pages=[https://archive.org/details/cultsofromanempi00robe/page/10 10–12]|url-access=registration}}. {{cite book|url =https://archive.org/details/oxfordenglishdic0006unse|title =Oxford English Dictionary|publisher =Oxford University Press|year =1989|edition =Second|location =Oxford, England|quote =The etymological meaning of L. superstitio is perhaps 'standing over a thing in amazement or awe.' Other interpretations of the literal meaning have been proposed, e.g., 'excess in devotion, over-scrupulousness or over-ceremoniousness in religion' and 'the survival of old religious habits in the midst of a new order of things'; but such ideas are foreign to ancient Roman thought.|url-access=registration}}</ref>

The earliest known use as a noun is found in [[Plautus]], [[Ennius]] and later in [[Pliny the Elder]], with the meaning of ''art of divination''.<ref>{{cite web|author =Manuela Simeoni|date =4 September 2011|title =Uso della parola superstitio contro i pagani|url =http://www.giornopaganomemoria.it/superstitio.html|language =it|access-date =3 August 2015|archive-date =19 November 2015|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20151119124403/http://www.giornopaganomemoria.it/superstitio.html|url-status =live}}</ref> From its use in the [[Classical Latin]] of [[Livy]] and [[Ovid]], it is used in the pejorative sense that it holds today: of an excessive fear of the gods or unreasonable religious belief; as opposed to ''[[:wikt:religio|religio]]'', the proper, reasonable awe of the gods. [[Cicero]] derived the term from ''superstitiosi'', lit. those who are "left over", i.e. "survivors", "descendants", connecting it with excessive anxiety of parents in hoping that their children would survive them to perform their necessary funerary rites.<ref>[[Cicero]], ''[[De Natura Deorum]]'' '''II''', '''28''' (32), quoted in {{cite book|last =Wagenvoort|first =Hendrik|url =https://archive.org/details/pietasselectedst0000wage/page/236|title =Pietas: selected studies in Roman religion|publisher =Brill|year =1980|isbn =978-90-04-06195-8|location =Leiden, Netherlands|page =[https://archive.org/details/pietasselectedst0000wage/page/236 236]|author-link =Hendrik Wagenvoort|url-access =registration}}</ref>

According to Michael David Bailey, it was with Pliny's usage that magic came close to superstition; and charges of being superstitious were first leveled by Roman authorities on their Christian subjects. In turn, early Christian writers saw all Roman and Pagan cults as superstitious, worshipping false Gods, fallen angels and demons. With Christian usage almost all forms of magic started being described as forms of superstition.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book|last =Bailey, Michael David, 1971–|title =Magic and superstition in Europe : a concise history from antiquity to the present|date =2007|publisher =Rowman & Littlefield Pub|isbn =978-0-7425-3386-8|location =Lanham|oclc =70267160}}
</ref>

==Superstition and psychology==
{{main|Magical thinking|Placebo|Effective theory}}

=== Origins ===
====Behaviorism perspective====
In 1948, behavioral psychologist [[B.F. Skinner]] published an article in the ''Journal of Experimental Psychology'', in which he described his [[pigeon]]s exhibiting what appeared to be superstitious behaviour. One pigeon was making turns in its cage, another would swing its head in a pendulum motion, while others also displayed a variety of other behaviours. Because these behaviors were all done ritualistically in an attempt to receive food from a dispenser, even though the dispenser had already been programmed to release food at set time intervals regardless of the pigeons' actions, Skinner believed that the pigeons were trying to influence their feeding schedule by performing these actions. He then extended this as a proposition regarding the nature of superstitious behavior in humans.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Skinner|first=B. F.|year=1948|url=http://psychclassics.yorku.ca/Skinner/Pigeon/|title='Superstition' in the Pigeon|journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology|volume=38|issue=2|pages=168–172|doi=10.1037/h0055873|pmid=18913665|s2cid=22577459|access-date=27 August 2007|archive-date=21 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191121090801/http://psychclassics.yorku.ca/Skinner/Pigeon/|url-status=live}}</ref>

Skinner's theory regarding superstition being the nature of the pigeons' behaviour has been challenged by other psychologists such as Staddon and Simmelhag, who theorised an alternative explanation for the pigeons' behaviour.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Staddon|first1=J. E.|name-list-style=amp|last2=Simmelhag|first2=V. L.|year=1971|title=The 'supersitition' experiment: A reexamination of its implications for the principles of adaptive behaviour|journal=Psychological Review|volume=78|issue=1|pages=3–43|doi=10.1037/h0030305}}</ref>

Despite challenges to Skinner's interpretation of the root of his pigeons' superstitious behaviour, his conception of the [[reinforcement|reinforcement schedule]] has been used to explain superstitious behaviour in humans. Originally, in Skinner's animal research, "some pigeons responded up to 10,000 times without reinforcement when they had originally been conditioned on an ''intermittent reinforcement'' basis."<ref name="schultz">Schultz & Schultz (2004, 238).{{full citation needed|date=November 2012}}</ref> Compared to the other reinforcement schedules (e.g., fixed ratio, fixed interval), these behaviours were also the most resistant to [[extinction (psychology)|extinction]].<ref name="schultz"/> This is called the ''partial reinforcement effect'', and this has been used to explain superstitious behaviour in humans. To be more precise, this effect means that, whenever an individual performs an action expecting a reinforcement, and none seems forthcoming, it actually creates a sense of persistence within the individual.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Carver|first1=Charles S.|last2=Scheier|first2=Michael|name-list-style=amp|title=Perspectives on personality|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tEHuAAAAMAAJ|year=2004|publisher=Allyn and Bacon|isbn=978-0-205-37576-9|page=332}}</ref>

==== Evolutionary/cognitive perspective ====
From a simpler perspective, [[natural selection]] will tend to reinforce a tendency to generate weak associations or [[Heuristics in judgment and decision-making|heuristics]] that are overgeneralized. If there is a strong survival advantage to making correct associations, then this will outweigh the negatives of making many incorrect, "superstitious" associations.<ref>{{cite journal|title=The evolution of superstitious and superstition-like behaviour |journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences|last1=Foster|first1=Kevin R.|last2=Kokko|first2=Hanna|author2-link=Hanna Kokko|doi=10.1098/rspb.2008.0981 |year=2009 |volume=276 |pmid=18782752 |issue=1654 |pmc=2615824 |pages=31–7}}</ref> It has also been argued that there may be connections between OCD and superstition.<ref>de Silva, Padmal and Rachman, Stanley (2004) ''Obsessive-compulsive Disorder'', Oxford University Press, p. 34, {{ISBN|0198520824}}.</ref> It is stated that superstitions are long-held beliefs that are rooted in coincidence and/or cultural tradition rather than logic and facts.<ref>{{Cite web |last=University |first=Manchester Metropolitan |title=Story, Manchester Metropolitan University |url=https://www.mmu.ac.uk/news-and-events/news/story/8081/#:~:text=Psychologists%20who%20have%20investigated%20what,protect%20you%20from%20bad%20luck. |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=Manchester Metropolitan University |language=en}}</ref>

OCD that involves superstition is often referred to as "Magical Thinking" <ref name=":9">{{Cite web |date=2024-01-20 |title=Magical Thinking OCD: Excessive Superstition |url=https://www.simplypsychology.org/magical-thinking.html |access-date=2024-07-02 |language=en-US}}</ref> People with this kind of manifestation of OCD believe that if they do not follow through with a certain compulsion, then something bad will happen to either themselves or others. Superstitious OCD, while can appear in anyone with OCD, more often appears in people with a religious background or with people who grew up in a culture that believes in magic and perform rituals.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Excessive Superstition In Cases Of OCD – Beyond OCD |url=https://beyondocd.org/expert-perspectives/articles/very-superstitious |access-date=2024-07-02 |language=en-US |archive-date=24 February 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240224152441/https://beyondocd.org/expert-perspectives/articles/very-superstitious |url-status=live }}</ref> Like stated before in the article above, superstition and prophecies are sometimes linked together. People with religious or superstitious OCD may have compulsions and perform ritualistic behaviors.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://kidshealth.org/en/teens/ocd.html | title=Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD) (For Teens) }}</ref><ref>{{Citation |last1=Mocan |first1=Naci H. |title=Can Superstition Create a Self-Fulfilling Prophecy? School Outcomes of Dragon Children of China |date=August 2017 |type=Working Paper |url=https://www.nber.org/papers/w23709 |access-date=2024-07-02 |series=Working Paper Series |doi=10.3386/w23709 |last2=Yu |first2=Han|doi-access=free }}</ref> Those with "magical thinking" OCD may realize that doing an action will not actually 'save' someone, but the fear that if they do not perform a certain behavior someone could get hurt is so overwhelming that they do it just to be sure. People with superstitious OCD will go out of their way to avoid something deemed 'unlucky'. Such as the 13th floor of a building, the 13th room, certain numbers or colors, because if they do not they believe something horrible may happen. Though superstitious OCD may work in reverse where one will always wear a certain item of clothing or jewelry or carry a certain item like a bag because it brings them 'luck' and allow good things to happen.<ref name=":9" />

A recent theory by Jane Risen proposes that superstitions are intuitions that people acknowledge to be wrong, but acquiesce to rather than correct when they arise as the intuitive assessment of a situation. Her theory draws on [[Dual process theory|dual-process]] models of reasoning. In this view, superstitions are the output of "System 1" reasoning that are not corrected even when caught by "System 2".<ref name=Risen>{{Cite journal|last=Risen|first=Jane L.|title=Believing what we do not believe: Acquiescence to superstitious beliefs and other powerful intuitions.|journal=Psychological Review|volume=123|issue=2|pages=182–207|doi=10.1037/rev0000017|pmid=26479707|year=2016|s2cid=14384232 }}</ref>

=== Mechanisms ===
People seem to believe that superstitions influence events by changing the likelihood of currently possible outcomes rather than by creating new possible outcomes. In sporting events, for example, a lucky ritual or object is thought to increase the chance that an athlete will perform at the peak of their ability, rather than increasing their overall ability at that sport.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Hamerman|first1=Eric J.|last2=Morewedge|first2=Carey K.|date=1 March 2015|title=Reliance on Luck Identifying Which Achievement Goals Elicit Superstitious Behavior|journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin|language=en|volume=41|issue=3|pages=323–335|doi=10.1177/0146167214565055|issn=0146-1672|pmid=25617118|s2cid=1160061}}</ref>

Psychologist [[Stuart Vyse]] has pointed out that until about 2010, "[m]ost researchers assumed superstitions were irrational and focused their attentions on discovering why people were superstitious." Vyse went on to describe studies that looked at the relationship between performance and superstitious rituals. Preliminary work has indicated that such rituals can reduce stress and thereby improve performance, but, Vyse has said, "...not because they are superstitious but because they are rituals.... So there is no real magic, but there is a bit of calming magic in performing a ritualistic sequence before attempting a high-pressure activity.... Any old ritual will do."<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Vyse|first1=Stuart|title=Do Superstitious Rituals Work?|journal=[[Skeptical Inquirer]]|date=2018|volume=42|issue=2|pages=32–34|url=https://www.csicop.org/specialarticles/show/do_superstitious_rituals_work|access-date=3 June 2018|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180603133012/https://www.csicop.org/specialarticles/show/do_superstitious_rituals_work |archive-date=3 June 2018}}</ref><ref name="VyseSI2020">{{cite journal |last1=Vyse |first1=Stuart |title=Obsessions and compulsions: Do superstitious rituals help cope with anxiety? |journal=[[Skeptical Inquirer]] |date=April 2020 |volume=44 |issue=2 |page=52}}</ref>

=== Occurrence ===
People tend to attribute events to supernatural causes (in psychological terms, "external causes") most often under two circumstances.
# People are more likely to attribute an event to a superstitious cause if it is unlikely than if it is likely. In other words, the more surprising the event, the more likely it is to evoke a supernatural explanation. This is believed to stem from an effectance motivation – a basic desire to exert control over one's environment. When no natural cause can explain a situation, attributing an event to a superstitious cause may give people some sense of control and ability to predict what will happen in their environment.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Waytz|first1=Adam|last2=Morewedge|first2=Carey K.|last3=Epley|first3=Nicholas|last4=Monteleone|first4=George|last5=Gao|first5=Jia-Hong|last6=Cacioppo|first6=John T.|title=Making sense by making sentient: Effectance motivation increases anthropomorphism.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=99|issue=3|pages=410–435|doi=10.1037/a0020240|pmid=20649365|year=2010|citeseerx=10.1.1.206.2736}}</ref>
# People are more likely to attribute an event to a superstitious cause if it is negative than positive. This is called ''[[Negativity bias#Attribution of Intentions|negative agency bias]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Morewedge|first=Carey K.|title=Negativity bias in attribution of external agency.|journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: General|volume=138|issue=4|pages=535–545|doi=10.1037/a0016796|pmid=19883135|year=2009|citeseerx=10.1.1.212.2333}}</ref> Boston Red Sox fans, for instance, attributed the failure of their team to win the world series for 86 years to the [[curse of the Bambino]]: a curse placed on the team for trading Babe Ruth to the New York Yankees so that the team owner could fund a Broadway musical. When the Red Sox finally won the world series in 2004, however, the team's success was attributed to the team's skill and the rebuilding effort of the new owner and general manager. More commonly, people are more likely to perceive their computer to act according to its own intentions when it malfunctions than functions properly.<ref name=":0" />

=== Consumer behavior ===
According to [[Consumer behaviour|consumer behavior]] analytics of John C. Mowen et al., superstitions are employed as a [[Heuristics in judgment and decision-making|heuristic tool]] hence those influence a variety of consumer behaviors.<ref name=":8">{{Cite journal|last1=Carlson|first1=Brad D.|last2=Mowen|first2=John C.|last3=Fang|first3=Xiang|date=2009|title=Trait superstition and consumer behavior: Re-conceptualization, measurement, and initial investigations|url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/mar.20295|journal=Psychology & Marketing|language=en|volume=26|issue=8|pages=689–713|doi=10.1002/mar.20295|issn=1520-6793|access-date=3 January 2021|archive-date=18 January 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210118174412/https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/mar.20295|url-status=live|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref name=":7" /> John C. Mowen et al. says, after taking into account for a set of antecedents, trait superstitions are predictive of a wide variety of consumer beliefs, like beliefs in astrology or in common negative superstitions (e.g., fear of black cats). A general proneness to be superstitious leads to enduring temperament to gamble, participation in promotional games, investments in stocks, forwarding of superstitious e‐mails, keeping good‐luck charms, and exhibit sport fanship etc.<ref name=":8" /><ref name=":7" /> Additionally it has been estimated that between $700 million and $800 million are lost every Friday the 13th because of people's refusal to travel, purchase major items or conduct business.<ref>{{Cite web |last=CNBC |date=13 August 2010 |title=Friday the 13th Means Millions in Lost Business, Productivity |url=https://www.cnbc.com/id/38691586 |access-date=2 June 2023 |website=[[CNBC]] |archive-date=2 June 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230602090756/https://www.cnbc.com/id/38691586 |url-status=live }}</ref> <!-- This journal article has been cited by fifty plus consumer behavior related journals and John C. Mowen seems to have been used as ref in some of other wikipedia articles earlier -->

==Superstition and politics==
[[Ancient Greece|Ancient Greek]] historian [[Polybius]] in his ''[[The Histories (Polybius)|Histories]]'' uses the word ''superstition'' explaining that in [[ancient Rome]] that belief maintained the cohesion of the [[Roman Empire|empire]], operating as an ''[[instrumentum regni]]''.<ref>Guy, Josephine M. (2007) ''The Complete Works of Oscar Wilde'', Oxford University Press, Vol. IV, p. 337, {{ISBN|0191568449}}.</ref>

==Opposition to superstition==
{{See also|Magical thinking}}
In the [[Classical antiquity|classical era]], the existence of gods was actively debated both among philosophers and theologians, and opposition to superstition arose consequently. The poem ''{{lang|la|[[De rerum natura]]}}'', written by the Roman poet and philosopher [[Lucretius]] further developed the opposition to superstition. [[Cicero]]'s work ''{{lang|la|[[De natura deorum]]}}'' also had a great influence on the development of the modern concept of superstition as well as the word itself. Where [[Cicero]] distinguished ''{{lang|la|superstitio}}'' and ''{{lang|la|religio}}'', [[Lucretius]] used only the word ''{{lang|la|religio}}''. [[Cicero]], for whom ''{{lang|la|superstitio}}'' meant "excessive fear of the gods" wrote that "''{{lang|la|superstitio, non religio, tollenda est}}'' ", which means that only superstition, and not religion, should be abolished. The [[Roman Empire]] also made laws condemning those who excited excessive religious fear in others.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.giornopaganomemoria.it/superstitio.html|title=Uso della parola superstitio contro i pagani|website=www.giornopaganomemoria.it|access-date=3 August 2015|archive-date=19 November 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151119124403/http://www.giornopaganomemoria.it/superstitio.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

During the Middle Ages, the idea of God's influence on the world's events went mostly undisputed. [[Trial by ordeal|Trials by ordeal]] were quite frequent, even though [[Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor|Frederick II]] (1194{{spnd}}1250{{nbsp}}{{abbr|AD|anno domini}}) was the first king who explicitly outlawed trials by ordeal as they were considered "irrational".<ref>{{cite web |title=Ma l'imperatore svevo fu conservatore o innovatore? |url=http://www.stupormundi.it/Houben1.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150429053347/http://www.stupormundi.it/Houben1.htm |archive-date=29 April 2015 }}</ref>

The rediscovery of lost classical works ([[The Renaissance]]) and scientific advancement led to a steadily increasing disbelief in superstition. A new, more [[rationalism|rationalistic]] lens was beginning to see use in exegesis. Opposition to superstition was central to the [[Age of Enlightenment]]. The first philosopher who dared to criticize superstition publicly and in a written form was [[Baruch Spinoza]], who was a key figure in the Age of Enlightenment.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Wilson |first1=Helen Judy |last2=Reill |first2=Peter Hanns |title=Encyclopedia of the Enlightenment |url=https://archive.org/details/encyclopediaenli00reil |url-access=limited |publisher=Facts on File |location=New York |isbn=978-0-8160-5335-3 |page=[https://archive.org/details/encyclopediaenli00reil/page/n595 577] |quote=.&nbsp;.&nbsp;.&nbsp;equating all Christian beliefs except those accessible to unaided reason with superstition&nbsp;.&nbsp;.&nbsp;.|year=2004 }}</ref>

== Regional and national superstitions ==
Most superstitions arose over the course of centuries and are rooted in regional and historical circumstances, such as religious beliefs or the natural environment. For instance, [[gecko]]s are believed to be of medicinal value in many Asian countries, including China.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Wagner|first1=P.|last2=Dittmann|first2=A.|date=2014|title=Medicinal use of Gekko gecko (Squamata: Gekkonidae) has an impact on agamid lizards.|url=http://salamandra-journal.com/index.php/home/contents/2014-vol-50/wagner-p-a-dittmann|journal=Salamandra|volume=50|pages=185–186|access-date=30 January 2020|archive-date=30 January 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200130003413/http://salamandra-journal.com/index.php/home/contents/2014-vol-50/wagner-p-a-dittmann|url-status=live}}</ref>

In China, [[Feng shui]] is a belief system that different places have negative effects, e.g. that a room in the northwest corner of a house is "very bad".<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://skepticalinquirer.org/exclusive/superstition-and-the-chinese-real-estate-market/|title=Superstition and the Chinese Real Estate Market {{!}} Skeptical Inquirer|last=Nicaise|first=Alexander|date=16 January 2020|language=en-US|access-date=29 January 2020|archive-date=27 January 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200127161529/https://skepticalinquirer.org/exclusive/superstition-and-the-chinese-real-estate-market/|url-status=live}}</ref> Similarly, the number 8 is a "[[Numerology#Chinese numerology|lucky number]]" in [[China]], so that it is more common than any other number in the Chinese housing market.<ref name=":1" />

=== Animals ===
[[File:Black cat crossing your path.jpg|A black cat|thumb]]
There are many different animals around the world that have been tied to superstitions. People in the West are familiar with the omen of a black cat crossing one's path.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Roud |first1=Stephen |title=The Penguin guide to the superstitions of Britain and Ireland |last2=Roud |first2=Stephen |date=2003 |publisher=Penguin Books |isbn=978-0-14-100673-4 |location=London |pages=67}}</ref> Locomotive engineers believe a hare crossing one's path is bad luck.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Cowan |first=James |date=1 September 1928 |title=The Romance of the Rail |journal=The New Zealand Railways Magazine |volume=3 |issue=5 |pages=36 |via=Victoria University of Wellington}}</ref> According to the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) the [[giant anteater]] (''Myrmecophaga tridactyla'') is targeted by motorists in regions of Brazil who do not want the creature to cross in front of them and give them bad luck.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The animals harmed by superstition {{!}} BBC Earth |url=https://www.bbcearth.com/news/the-animals-harmed-by-superstition |access-date=28 July 2022 |website=www.bbcearth.com |language=en |archive-date=28 July 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220728043210/https://www.bbcearth.com/news/the-animals-harmed-by-superstition |url-status=live }}</ref>

The [[northern cardinal]] (''Cardinalis cardinalis'') is associated with receiving visits from heaven according to old folklore.<ref name=MNO /> With its name linked to [[Cardinal (Catholicism)|cardinals]] of the [[Roman Catholic Church]],<ref>{{cite book|last= Holloway|first= Joel Ellis|title= Dictionary of Birds of the United States: Scientific and Common Names|publisher= [[Workman Publishing Company|Timber Press]]|year= 2003|pages= 59|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=41knpiVqnKYC&q=Cathartes+aura+subject:%22Nature+/+Birds+%26+Birdwatching%22&pg=PA59|isbn= 0-88192-600-0}}</ref> sightings of the symbolic bird indicate positivity and hope, as described in the quote "''Cardinals appear when Angels are near''".<ref name="MNO">{{cite web |last1=Hobson |first1=Donald |title=The Facts and Myths About Cardinal Birds |url=https://www.marylandnature.org/the-facts-and-myths-about-cardinal-birds/ |website=www.marylandnature.org |access-date=1 May 2025 |date=March 21, 2021}}</ref>

=== Numbers ===
Certain numbers hold significance for particular cultures and communities. It is common for buildings to omit certain floors on their elevator panels and there are specific terms for people with severe aversions to specific numbers.<ref>{{Cite web |title=No more skipping 4, 13, 14, 24 in Vancouver floor numbers |url=https://vancouversun.com/news/local-news/no-more-skipping-4-13-14-24-in-vancouver-floor-numbers |access-date=28 July 2022 |website=vancouversun |language=en-CA |archive-date=12 November 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201112042029/https://vancouversun.com/news/local-news/no-more-skipping-4-13-14-24-in-vancouver-floor-numbers |url-status=live }}</ref> [[Triskaidekaphobia]], for example, is the fear of the number 13.<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 September 2013 |title=Superstitious Numbers Around the World |url=https://www.nationalgeographic.com/culture/article/130913-friday-luck-lucky-superstition-13 |access-date=28 July 2022 |website=Culture |language=en |archive-date=30 June 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220630050705/https://www.nationalgeographic.com/culture/article/130913-friday-luck-lucky-superstition-13 |url-status=dead }}</ref> Similarly, the number 4 is avoided in many contexts in East Asia, due to the (near-)homophony between the words {{Lang|zh|四}} "four" ([[Mandarin Chinese|Mandarin]] ''sì'') and {{Lang|zh|死}} "death" (''sǐ'') in Chinese and [[Sino-Xenic vocabularies|languages influenced by it]]. This is known as [[tetraphobia]] ({{ety|grc|''τετράς'' (tetrás)|four|grc|''φόβος'' (phóbos)|fear}}). A widespread superstition is fear of the number 666, given as the [[number of the beast]] in the biblical [[Book of Revelation]]. This fear is called hexakosioihexekontahexaphobia.

=== Objects ===
There are many objects tied to superstitions. During the Great Depression, it was common for people to carry a rabbit's foot around with them.<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |author1=Natalie Wolchover |date=19 September 2011 |title=The Surprising Origins of 9 Common Superstitions |url=https://www.livescience.com/33507-origins-of-superstitions.html |access-date=28 July 2022 |website=livescience.com |language=en |archive-date=27 November 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201127014317/https://www.livescience.com/33507-origins-of-superstitions.html |url-status=live }}</ref> During the [[COVID-19 pandemic|Coronavirus pandemic]], people in parts of Indonesia made ''tetek melek,'' a traditional homemade mask made of coconut palm fronds, which was hung in doorways to keep occupants safe.{{citation needed|date=March 2023}}

According to superstitions, breaking a [[mirror]] is said to bring seven years of [[List of bad luck signs|bad luck]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.mirrorhistory.com/mirror-facts/broken-mirror/|title=Breaking a mirror – meaning of broken mirror|work=Mirror History|access-date=12 April 2017|archive-date=13 April 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170413072419/http://www.mirrorhistory.com/mirror-facts/broken-mirror/|url-status=live}}</ref> From ancient Rome to Northern India, mirrors have been handled with care, or sometimes avoided all together.<ref name=":4" />

[[Horseshoe|Horseshoes]] have long been considered lucky. Opinion is divided as to which way up the horseshoe ought to be nailed. Some say the ends should point up, so that the horseshoe catches the luck, and that the ends pointing down allow the good luck to be lost; others say they should point down, so that the luck is poured upon those entering the home.
Superstitious sailors believe that nailing a horseshoe to the mast will help their vessel avoid storms.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.indepthinfo.com/horseshoes/luck.htm |title=Luck and Horseshoes |publisher=Indepthinfo.com |access-date=19 December 2011 |archive-date=29 October 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191029135942/http://www.indepthinfo.com/horseshoes/luck.htm |url-status=dead }}</ref>

In China, yarrow and tortoiseshell are considered lucky and brooms have a number of superstitions attached to them. It is considered bad luck to use a broom within three days of the new year as this will sweep away good luck.<ref name="us.mofcom.gov.cn">{{Cite web |title=Chinese customs, superstitions and traditions |url=http://us.mofcom.gov.cn/article/aboutchina/202011/20201103012645.shtml#:~:text=Other%20customs%20and%20superstitions%20include,and%201pm%20quarrels%20will%20ensue. |access-date=28 July 2022 |website=us.mofcom.gov.cn |archive-date=28 July 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220728043211/http://us.mofcom.gov.cn/article/aboutchina/202011/20201103012645.shtml#:~:text=Other%20customs%20and%20superstitions%20include,and%201pm%20quarrels%20will%20ensue. |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Actions ===
Common actions in the West include not walking under a ladder, knocking on wood, throwing salt over one's shoulder after one spills a container, or not opening an umbrella indoors. In China wearing certain colours is believed to bring luck.<ref name="us.mofcom.gov.cn"/>

[[Break a leg|"Break a leg"]] is a typical English [[idiom]] used in the context of [[theatre]] or other [[performing arts]] to wish a performer "[[luck|good luck]]". An ironic or non-literal saying of uncertain origin (a [[dead metaphor]]), "break a leg" is commonly said to [[actor]]s and [[musician]]s before they go on stage to perform or before an [[audition]]. In English (though it may originate in German), the expression was likely first used in this context in the United States in the 1930s or possibly 1920s,<ref name="WWWords">{{Cite web |url=http://www.worldwidewords.org/qa/qa-bre1.htm |title=Break a Leg |publisher=World Wide Words |access-date=24 April 2007 |archive-date=3 April 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070403165551/http://www.worldwidewords.org/qa/qa-bre1.htm |url-status=live }}</ref> originally documented without specifically theatrical associations. Among [[Dance|professional dancers]], the traditional saying is not "break a leg", but the French word "''[[Shit#"Merde" for "good luck"|merde]]''".<ref name=McConnell1977>{{cite book |last1=McConnell |first1=Joan |last2=McConnell |first2=Teena |title=Ballet as body language |url=https://archive.org/details/balletasbodylang00mcco_0 |url-access=registration |publisher=Harper & Row |year=1977 |isbn=0-06-012964-6}}</ref>

Some superstitious actions have practical origins. Opening an umbrella inside in eighteenth-century London was a physical hazard, as umbrellas then were metal-spoked, clumsy spring mechanisms and a "veritable hazard to open indoors."<ref>{{Cite book |last=Panati |first=Charles |title=Panati's extraordinary origins of everyday things |date=1989 |isbn=0-06-096419-7 |location=New York |oclc=20521056}}</ref>

Another superstition with practical origins is the action of blowing briefly left and right before crossing rail tracks for safe travels as the person engaging in the action looks both ways.<ref>{{Cite web |title=TrackSAFE – A rail safety superstition |url=https://www.tracksafe.co.nz/safety/campaigns/a-rail-safety-superstition |access-date=28 July 2022 |website=tracksafe.co.nz |archive-date=18 January 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230118041801/https://www.tracksafe.co.nz/safety/campaigns/a-rail-safety-superstition |url-status=live }}</ref>
